nativeRange.habitat: Large flowing rivers and flooded fields; takes refuge among submerged rocks and tree roots during daylight hours; strongly migratory, undertaking seasonal upstream and downstream spawning migrations tied to water-level changes

decorPreferences: driftwood and root tangles for refuge
caves and overhangs (PVC tubes, rock piles)
moderate current to replicate river conditions
open swimming corridors between shelter zones

diet.notes: Opportunistic bottom-feeder with a strong bias toward meaty and invertebrate foods. Naturally forages for aquatic snails, worms, insect larvae, and small crustaceans — an effective snail-control species. Accepts high-quality sinking pellets, frozen bloodworm, Tubifex, brine shrimp, daphnia, and chopped earthworms; also takes blanched vegetables (cucumber, zucchini, spinach) and algae wafers. Feed after lights-out to suit its nocturnal foraging habit. Offer variety; mono-diets lead to nutritional deficiency.

goodWith: large active cyprinids (tiger barbs, tinfoil barbs, giant danios)
other mid-to-upper-water fish of similar or larger size
robust cichlids of comparable size (with caution)
avoidWith: small fish that can be bullied or eaten
other bottom-dwellers competing for the same territory and food
invertebrates — shrimp and snails will be consumed
peaceful community fish unsuited to semi-aggressive tankmates

summary: A large, blue-bodied loach from Southeast Asian river systems, distinguished by its vivid orange-red caudal fin. A gregarious shoaler requiring groups of 5 or more, an effective natural snail controller, and an intermediate-level fish owing to its size (up to 25 cm), territory demands, and semi-aggressive disposition.
careNotes:
The Redtail Botia was long traded as Botia modesta; the genus Yasuhikotakia was erected by Nalbant (2002) and further consolidated by Kottelat (2004), separating this and related species from the polyphyletic Botia sensu lato. The name Botia modesta still circulates widely in the trade and older literature. A second synonym, Botia rubripinnis, describes the same species.

This is a large, robust loach reaching up to 25 cm total length in the wild, though aquarium specimens typically top out at 18–22 cm. A tank of at least 300 L with a 150 cm footprint is the accepted minimum for a shoal of five to six adults; larger is strongly recommended. Tight social groups of at least five — ideally ten or more — are essential: undersized groups intensify conspecific aggression and stress, while a larger shoal distributes the pecking-order hierarchy and produces calmer, bolder fish.

The species is nocturnal by nature, spending daylight hours concealed within caves, root tangles, or rock piles. Dense structured decor with multiple retreat zones is mandatory; bare or sparsely decorated tanks cause chronic stress. A moderate current mimicking the riverine habitat is beneficial. Sand or smooth fine gravel (minimum 5 cm deep) is preferred — sharp substrate damages the barbels.

The Redtail Botia is a reliable snail-eater and is often introduced specifically to control pest snail populations (Melanoides, ramshorns, pond snails). Invertebrates including shrimp should not be kept with this species.

The species is sensitive to poor water quality and to standard copper-based medications (as with all scaleless loaches). Maintain good filtration and stable parameters. Quarantine new specimens carefully — ich and other external parasites are common on imports, and half-doses of medication are advised for treatment. Wild individuals are migratory seasonal spawners; captive breeding has not been reliably achieved without hormone induction.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding is considered practically impossible under normal aquarium conditions. In the wild, Yasuhikotakia modesta undertakes extensive seasonal migrations — moving upstream November–March and back downstream May–July in the lower Mekong system — to spawn in flooded forests and riverside fields during the rainy season. Commercial aquaculture production relies on hormonal induction. No reliable reports of private aquarist breeding exist. Sexing is difficult outside of spawning condition; ripe females may appear noticeably broader in the abdomen.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Yasuhikotakia modesta
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (200–250 mm SL), temperature (23–28°C), pH (6.0–8.0), hardness (36–215 ppm ≈ 2–12 dGH), native range (Thailand, Laos, Cambodia, Vietnam; Chao Phraya, Mae Klong, Mekong drainages), tank minimum (150 × 45 cm base), habitat, diet, breeding notes on migratory spawning, and social requirements (groups of 5–6 minimum, 10+ preferred). Notes Botia modesta and Botia rubripinnis as synonyms; cites Nalbant 2002 reclassification to Yasuhikotakia.

additional.name: FishBase — Yasuhikotakia modesta
additional.notes: Cross-checked maximum size (25 cm SL), temperature (26–30°C), taxonomy (Botiidae, Cypriniformes), native range (Mekong, Chao Phraya, Mae Khlong basins), habitat (large rivers with muddy substrate, flooded fields), diet (nocturnal predator: worms, crustaceans, insects), and captive lifespan (8+ years reported). Confirmed strongly migratory behaviour.

additional.name: Fish Laboratory — Blue Botia (Yasuhikotakia modesta)
additional.notes: Supplementary care guide. Confirmed temperature 22–30°C, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness 8–12 dGH, minimum 75-gallon tank (≈284 L) for adults, shoal of 5–10 required, lifespan approximately 5 years in captivity with proper care, sensitivity to medications (scaleless fish), compatibility guidance (large active cyprinids only).

additional.name: Tropical Fish Keeping — Redtail Botia Loach
additional.notes: Confirmed Yasuhikotakia modesta as accepted name (formerly Botia modesta), native range lower Mekong basin plus Chao Phraya and Mae Klong drainages, semi-aggressive temperament, groups of 6–8+ minimum, lifespan 5–7 years, diet (snails, invertebrates, insects, worms as natural prey), not suitable for peaceful community tanks, commercial hormone breeding.

additional.name: AquaInfo — Yasuhikotakia modesta Redtail Botia
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size up to 24 cm, temperature 24–28°C, pH 6.0–7.0, minimum tank length 150 cm, groups of at least 6, synonyms Botia modesta and Botia rubripinnis, native range including Chao Phraya, Great Lake (Tonle Sap), Mae Klong, Mekong, and Tonle Sap ecosystems.

fish.breedingNotes: Seasonal migratory spawner; captive breeding without hormonal induction is unknown. Commercial aquaculture uses hormone injection. Wild fish migrate extensively to spawn in flooded forests during the rainy season. Sexing is difficult outside of breeding condition.
